hub_subtitle: Business English para as reuniões com o time Global da Sanofi, congressos médicos e a apresentação de agosto

lesson.menu_title: How Is It Going? -- Reporting Progress to the Global Team
lesson.menu_desc: O check-in de meio de caminho: quantos centros estão on track, quanto progresso há de verdade, e o que ainda falta antes de agosto -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- How Is It Going?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Emmanuele Orrico | Aula 6 -- How Is It Going?

listenings.text: Emmanuele, Marc Dubois. In August I am going to ask you one question, and I want you to practice the answer now. The question is: how is it going? Not the plan. The plan I already have. I want the progress. So, before our call on Friday, please send me a short update. Three things only. How many centers are on track, and how many are late. How much progress there is in the private market. And any problem that you are not going to solve alone. Please, do not send me forty slides. I do not have much time, and neither do you. Three lines are enough. Talk to you Friday.

Good morning, everyone. Claire Bennett here, with the weekly update from the global immunology team. First, the good news. The dermatology campaign is on track in eleven countries, and we are rolling it out in four more this month. There is a lot of progress in the private market, and the first milestone is complete. Now the difficult part. We have a few delays in the public market, because there is not much information about the reimbursement decision yet. Some countries are still waiting. So, if you are tracking a campaign in your country, please send me two numbers before Friday: how many centers are active, and how many doctors your team is visiting this month. Are there any questions? Write to me. Thank you, everyone.

extra_audio.text: Good morning, Marc. Here is the update on the dermatology campaign. The first milestone is complete, and we are on track. We have a lot of progress in the private market: eleven centers are active. But we have a few delays in the public market, because there is not much information about the reimbursement yet. So far, so good.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: an important point in a project, where you check if you are on time
to follow the numbers of a project, to see where it is
an organized set of actions to bring a medicine to doctors
happening now, and not finished yet
new information about the situation of a project
going well -- at the right speed, at the right time
the movement forward in a project
to start something step by step, in more and more places

paras: Every manager knows how to write a plan. A plan is comfortable: it lives in the future, and in the future nothing has gone wrong yet. But a plan is not what a global team asks for in the middle of the year. They ask a much harder question: how is it going?

A good progress update is short and honest, and it has three parts. First, the things you can count: how many centers are active, how many doctors your team is visiting, how many milestones are complete. Second, the things you cannot count one by one: how much progress there is in the market, how much time you still have, how much information is still missing. And third, the problems -- because a manager who reports only good news is a manager nobody believes.

The mistake is to send forty slides. Forty slides say: I am busy. Three lines say: I am in control. So keep it simple. "The campaign is on track in eleven centers. We have a lot of progress in the private market. We have a few delays in the public market, and there is not much information about the reimbursement yet." That is a professional update, and it is only three sentences long.

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: A global team wants a long presentation with forty slides, because more detail is always better.
A good progress update is short and honest: the things you can count, the things you cannot count, and the problems.
A manager should report only good news, so that the global team stays confident.

items: The text says a plan is harder to write than a progress update.
The opposite: "A plan is comfortable." The harder question is "how is it going?" -- the progress, not the plan.
A good update has three parts: what you can count, what you cannot count, and the problems.
The second paragraph lists exactly these three parts.
The text says you should report only the good news.
It says the opposite: "a manager who reports only good news is a manager nobody believes."
According to the text, forty slides make you look in control.
"Forty slides say: I am busy. Three lines say: I am in control."

parts: A good update is short. Start with what you can count: "
centers are active?" Then the things you cannot count one by one: "
progress is there in the private market?" Say the good news -- "We have
progress this month" -- and then the honest part: "We have
delays in the public market, and there is not

gapfill.text: Notice the two families. Things you can count one by one -- centers, doctors, milestones, delays -- take HOW MANY and A FEW. Things you cannot count one by one -- progress, time, information, news -- take HOW MUCH and A LITTLE. And A LOT OF works with both: it is your safety net.

lf.title: Can you count it, or not?
items: progress is there in the private market?

lf.text: A and C count things one by one: one center, two centers; one delay, two delays. They take many and a few. B and D cannot be counted one by one: you never say "two progresses" or "three informations". They take much and a little. In Portuguese, "muito" is one single word for both -- and that is exactly why this is worth five minutes of your life.

bank.label: Useful language for a progress update
items: Here is a short update on the dermatology campaign.
The first milestone is complete, and we are on track.
We have a lot of progress in the private market.
Eleven centers are active, and we are rolling it out in four more.
We have a few delays in the public market.
There is not much information about the reimbursement yet.

items.situation: Marc opens the call: "So, how is it going?" Give him the headline in one sentence.
tips: The campaign is on track. So far, so good.
Here is a short update: the first milestone is complete, and we are on track.
Eleven centers are active, and we are rolling it out in four more this month.
We have eleven active centers at the moment.

items.situation: He asks about the private market. Report the good news, and say it is a big amount.
tips: We have a lot of progress in the private market.
There is a lot of progress in the private market, and the first milestone is complete.

items.situation: Now the honest part: there are two or three delays in the public market. Report them without drama.
tips: We have a few delays in the public market.
There are a few delays in the public market, and my team is tracking them.

items.situation: He asks: "Do you have the reimbursement information?" You have almost none. Say it professionally.
tips: There is not much information about the reimbursement yet.
We have very little information about the reimbursement at the moment.

items.situation: You want to know if he has anything to tell you before the call ends. Ask him.
tips: Are there any updates from your side?
Do you have any questions, or any news from Paris?
